\chapter{Thermodynamic cycles and heat engines}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:thermodynamic-cycles-and-heat-engines}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Direct and reversed thermodynamic cycles.} Direct cycles: transform heat to mechanical work. Reverset cycle: transform mechanical work to heat transfer (e.g. refrigeration/cooling \sphinxhyphen{} refrigerator cycles \sphinxhyphen{} or heating \sphinxhyphen{} heat pump cycles)

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Carnot cycle \sphinxhyphen{} reversible cycle between two constant temperatur sources.} Planck and Kelvin statements of \(2^{nd}\) principle of thermodynamics. Maximum efficiency….

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Ideal models of real cycles.} Otto, Diesel, Atkinson, Stirling (ICE), Rankine, Joule\sphinxhyphen{}Brayton

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Principle.} Reciprocating (piston)/reactive (turbine) engines

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Circuit.} Open/close


\section{Carnot cycle and heat engine}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:carnot-cycle-and-heat-engine}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-carnot}}

\subsection{Second principle of thermodynamics: Planck and Kelvin statements}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:second-principle-of-thermodynamics-planck-and-kelvin-statements}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:id1}}

\section{Heat engines and cycles}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:heat-engines-and-cycles}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-real}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this section, the idealization/model of real thermodynamic cycles performed by real\sphinxhyphen{}life machines are discussed.


\subsection{Otto}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:otto}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-real-otto}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Introduction.}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Applications.}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Thermodynamic cycle.} Reciprocating operation, with (approximately%
\begin{footnote}[1]\sphinxAtStartFootnote
Combustion chamber is an approximately close system during a cycle between intake and exhaust, because of: 1. fuel injection, 2. leakage through the clearance between the walls of the piston and the cylinder.
%
\end{footnote}) close system between intake and exhaust processes:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(0 \rightarrow 1\) intake, approximately isobaric

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(1 \rightarrow 2\) adiabatic compression

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(2 \rightarrow 3\) combustion, approximately isochoric at TDC: \sphinxstylestrong{spark ignition} usually makes combustion fast enough to be modeled as an immediate process occurring at TDC; this is in contast to {\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-real-diesel}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{Diesel engines}}}}, where combustion starts with self\sphinxhyphen{}ignition due to high temperature reached at the end of the compression

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(3 \rightarrow 4\) adiabatic expansion

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(4 \rightarrow 0\) exhaust, approximately isochoric BDC followed by isobaric

\end{itemize}


\subsection{Diesel}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:diesel}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-real-diesel}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Introduction.}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Applications.}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Thermodynamic cycle.} Reciprocating operation, with (approximately\sphinxfootnotemark[1]) close system between intake and exhaust processes:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(0 \rightarrow 1\) intake, approximately isobaric

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(1 \rightarrow 2\) adiabatic compression

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(2 \rightarrow 3\) combustion, approximately isobaric at TDC: \sphinxstylestrong{self ignition} usually triggers slower combustion if compared with {\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-real-otto}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{Otto engines}}}}, that can be modeled as an isobaric transformation

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(3 \rightarrow 4\) adiabatic expansion

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(4 \rightarrow 0\) exhaust, approximately isochoric BDC followed by isobaric

\end{itemize}


\subsection{Joule\sphinxhyphen{}Brayton}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:joule-brayton}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-real-joule-brayton}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Introduction.} It’s the reference thermodynamic cycle for \sphinxstylestrong{gas turbine} heat engines. These systems have continuous operation and can work both in open (jet propulsion) and close cycles (electric power generation).

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Applications.} Joule\sphinxhyphen{}Brayton close cycle is used in electric power generation: heat producesd by combustion is transferred to an operating fluid(chemical power to thermal power), entnering a turbine that converts “thermal power of the fluid” into mechanical power, then converted to electric power by an electric generator. Joule\sphinxhyphen{}Brayton open cycle is used for jet propulsion (typically for aircraft): operating fluid is usually air, undergoing the thermodynamic cylce through the jet engine; air is compressed before entering the combustion chamber, and released after passing throught a turbine: turbine extracts the power required to drive the compressor, and “extra power” contained in the fluid is used to accelerating exhaust fluid, producing thrust.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Thermodynamic cycle \sphinxhyphen{} close cycle.} Components involved in the system are usually open systems. Steady operation of these systems is described by balance of total enthalpy flux. Kinetic energy of the fhe fluid is usually negligible if compared with internal energy and enthapy: total entalphy reduces to \sphinxstylestrong{enthalpy}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(1 \rightarrow 2\) adiabatic compression

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(2 \rightarrow 3\) combustion, approximately isobaric at in combustion chamber

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(3 \rightarrow 4\) adiabatic expansion

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(4 \rightarrow 1\) isobaric cooling

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Thermodynamic cycle \sphinxhyphen{} open cycle.} Components involved in the system are usually open systems. Steady operation of these systems is described by balance of \sphinxstylestrong{total enthalpy} flux. Kinetic energy of the fhe fluid is usually \sphinxstylestrong{not} negligible if compared with internal energy and enthapy
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(0 \rightarrow 1\) adiabatic compression in free air before or at the engine intake

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(1 \rightarrow 2\) adiabatic compression in compressor

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(2 \rightarrow 3\) combustion, approximately isobaric at in combustion chamber

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(3 \rightarrow 4\) adiabatic expansion in turbine

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(4 \rightarrow 5\) adiabatic expansion in the nozzle

\end{itemize}


\subsection{Rankine}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:rankine}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-real-rankine}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Introduction.}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Application.}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Thermodynamic cycle.}


\section{Applications}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:applications}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-systems}}

\subsection{Mechanical power}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:mechanical-power}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-systems-engine}}

\subsection{Power generation}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:power-generation}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-systems-power-generation}}

\subsection{Heat pump and/or refrigeration}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:heat-pump-and-or-refrigeration}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-engines:classical-thermodynamics-heat-engines-systems-heat-pump-refrigeration}}

\chapter{Heat transfer}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer:heat-transfer}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Three main heat transfer mechanisms exist: conduction, convection, radiation.

\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-conduction:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-conduction}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{\sphinxstylestrong{Conduction}}}}}. Main heat transfer mechanism in solid media: molecules of the solid are not free to move and conduction is a diffusion process of the microscopical thermal agitation of the molecules of the solid structure.

\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-convection:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-convection}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{\sphinxstylestrong{Convection}}}}}. Main heat transfer machanism in fluid media: moldeculus of the fluid are free to move; as they move, they transport “thermal energy” and xechange heat with the surrounding environment.

\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-radiation}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{\sphinxstylestrong{Radiation}}}}}. It’s the only heat transfer mechanism that needs no matter to occur, as it can occur in “vacuum”%
\begin{footnote}[1]\sphinxAtStartFootnote
Vacuum of matter, mass, but not physical properties, as the dielectric constant and magnetic permeability of free space/”vacuum”.
%
\end{footnote}


\bigskip\hrule\bigskip


\sphinxstepscope


\section{Conduction}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-conduction:conduction}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-conduction:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-conduction}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-conduction::doc}}\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  \rho c \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
  & = - \nabla \cdot \vec{q} + \rho r \\
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Fourier model of heat condcution flux} wants the heat flux to be proportional to the temperature gradient, \(\nabla T\). The most general expression for linear non\sphinxhyphen{}isotropic media reads
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\vec{q} = - \mathbb{K} \cdot \nabla T \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
with \(\mathbb{K}\) the conductivity tensor (a second\sphinxhyphen{}oreder (semi)definite (symmetric? \sphinxstylestrong{todo} \sphinxstyleemphasis{check, Onsager reciprocal relations}) positive to comply with the second principle of thermodynamics: “heat transfers energy from hot to cold regions”). The behavior of isotorpic media is described by isotropic tensors; thus%
\begin{footnote}[1]\sphinxAtStartFootnote
Isotrpic 2\sphinxhyphen{}nd order tensors are the identity tensor and its multiples, \(\mathbb{A}^{iso} = a \mathbb{I}\). The dot product of 2\sphinxhyphen{}nd order identity tensor and a vector (field) gives the vector itself, \(\mathbb{I} \cdot  \vec{v} = \vec{v}\).
%
\end{footnote} the conductivity tensor becomes \(\mathbb{K}^{iso} = k \mathbb{I}\), and the heat flux reads
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\vec{q} = - k \nabla T \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Differential problem.} It follows that conduction in a … medium in region \(\Omega\) for time \(t \in [t_0, t_1]\) is governed by the differential problem represented by the PDE
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  \rho c \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
  & = \nabla \cdot \left( k \, \nabla T \right) + \rho r \qquad \vec{r} \in \Omega \ ,
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
supplied with proper initial conditions in every point \(\vec{r} \in \Omega\)
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}T(\vec{r}, t_0) = T_0(\vec{r}) \qquad \vec{r} \in \Omega\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
and boundary conditions for every point on the boudary of the domain, \(\vec{r} \in \partial \Omega\) for every time \(t \in [t_0, t_1]\). Different boundary conditions may represent different physical processes:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
known temperature on \(S_D\) (Dirichlet boundary conditions: the value of the unknown function is prescribed),
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}T(\vec{r}, t) = T_D (\vec{r}, t) \qquad \vec{r} \in S_D\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
known heat flux on \(S_N\) (Neumann boundary conditions: the value of the directional derivative of the unknown function is prescribed, in the normal direction w.r.t. the boundary),
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\hat{n}(\vec{r},t) \cdot \nabla T(\vec{r}, t) = \phi_N (\vec{r}, t) \qquad \vec{r} \in S_N\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
known value of a linear combination of the value of the unknown function and its directional derivative (Robin boundary conditions),
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\alpha T(\vec{r},t) + \beta \hat{n}(\vec{r},t) \cdot \nabla T(\vec{r}, t) = h(\vec{r},t) \qquad \vec{r} \in S_R\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{todo}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
derivation of this equation from governing equations of continuum mechanics: list assumptions, thermodynamic conditions (to transform internal energy \(e\) as a function of \(T\))

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
link to \sphinxstyleemphasis{Mathematics:Numerical methods for PDEs:Elliptic (steady) and Parabolic (unsteady) problems}

\end{itemize}




\bigskip\hrule\bigskip


\sphinxstepscope


\section{Convection}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-convection:convection}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-convection:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-convection}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-convection::doc}}
\sphinxstepscope


\section{Radiation}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:radiation}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-radiation}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
check all the nomenclature/definition


\subsection{Planck’s law}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:planck-s-law}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-radiation-planck}}\label{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:definition-0}
\begin{sphinxadmonition}{note}{Definition 9.3.1 (Black\sphinxhyphen{}body)}


\end{sphinxadmonition}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Spectral energy density} for black\sphinxhyphen{}body radiation
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}u_{f}(f, T) = \frac{8 \pi h f^3}{c^3} \frac{1}{e^{\frac{hf}{k_B T}} - 1}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Spectral energy density has the physical dimension \(\frac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Volume} \times \text{Frequency}}\)

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Spectral radiance} reads%
\begin{footnote}[1]\sphinxAtStartFootnote
Show how to get this expression from the spectral energy density.
%
\end{footnote} \(B_f = \frac{1}{4 \pi} u_f(f,T) c\) and thus for a black\sphinxhyphen{}body
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}B_{f}(f, T) = \frac{2 h f^3}{c^2}\frac{1}{e^{\frac{hf}{k_B T}} - 1} \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
It has physical dimension \(\frac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Volume} \times \text{Frequency}} \times \text{Velocity} = \frac{\text{Power}}{\text{Area} \times \text{Frequency}}\).


\subsection{Wien’s law}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:wien-s-law}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-radiation-wien}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Wien’s law states that the maximum of the spectral energy is obtained at a frequency \(f^*\), whose value is proportional with the temperature of the body.
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}f^* = x^* \frac{k_B}{h} T \simeq 2.82  \frac{k_B}{h} T \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
where \(x^* \simeq 2.82\) is the solution of the algebraic equation \(3(1-e^x)-x=0\).
\subsubsection*{Proof of Wien’s law}

\sphinxAtStartPar
From direct evaluation of the derivative of the spectral radiance as a function of \(f\),
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  \partial_f B_{f}(f,T)
  & = \frac{2 h}{c^2} \left[ 3 f^2 \frac{1}{e^{\frac{hf}{k_B T}}-1} + f^3 \left(-\frac{\frac{h}{k_B T} e^{\frac{hf}{k_B T}}}{\left( e^{\frac{hf}{k_B T}} - 1 \right)^2}  \right) \right] = \\
  & = \frac{2 h f^2 e^{\frac{hf}{k_B T}}}{c^2 \left( e^{\frac{hf}{k_B T}} - 1 \right)^2} \left[ 3 \left( 1 - e^{-\frac{hf}{k_B T}} \right) - \frac{h f}{k_B T}  \right] \ .
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Now, if \(\partial_f B_{f}(f,T) = 0\) the frequency is either \(f = 0\), or the solution of the nonlinear algebraic equation
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}0 = 3 \left(1 - e^{-\frac{h f}{k_B T}} \right) - \frac{hf}{k_B T} \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Defining \(x := \frac{h f}{k_B T}\), this equation becomes
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}0 = 3 (1 - e^x) - x \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
whose solution \(x^* \approx 2.82\) can be easily evaluated with an iterative method (or expressed in term of the Lambert’s function \(W\), so loved at Stanford and on Youtube: they’d probaly like to look at tabulated values, or pose). Once the solution \(x^*\) of this non\sphinxhyphen{}dimensional equation is found, the frequency where maximum energy density occurs reads
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}f^* = \frac{k_B T}{h} x^* \simeq 2.82 \frac{k_B}{h} T \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}

\subsection{Energy transfer by radiation}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:energy-transfer-by-radiation}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-radiation-energy-transfer}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A list of physical quantities used to describe a radiation process follows.
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Radiant flux}, \(\Phi_e\). Physical dimension: \(\text{W}\)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Radiant intensity} \(I_{e, \Omega}\) is the radiant flux per unit solid angle \(\Omega\). Physical dimension: \(\frac{\text{W}}{\text{sr}}\)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Spectral radiant intensity} \(I_{e, \Omega, f}\) is the radiant flux per unit solid angle per frequency of the radiation: \(\frac{\text{W}}{\text{sr} \, \text{Hz}}\)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Radiant exitance}, \(M_e\) is the flux across an emispherical receiver surface, per unit surface of the source. Physical dimension \(\frac{\text{W}}{\text{Area}}\)

\end{itemize}
\label{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:definition-1}
\begin{sphinxadmonition}{note}{Definition 9.3.2 (Lambert medium)}



\sphinxAtStartPar
A Lambert medium is defined as a medium that follows \sphinxstylestrong{Lambert cosine law},
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}I_{1}(\vec{r}_{12}, \hat{n}_1) = I_{1,0} \, \hat{r}_{12} \cdot \hat{n}_1 \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
with \(\vec{r}_{12} = \vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1\).
\end{sphinxadmonition}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Elementary power flux of the radiation emitted by a source in \(1\) through a solid angle \(d \Omega_{21}\), as seen from \(1\), reads
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  d P_{1 \rightarrow 2}
  & = I_{1}(\vec{r}_{12}, \hat{n}_1) \, d \Omega_{2/1} \\
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The solid angle \(d \Omega_{21}\) of a surface \(d S_2\) as seen from a point in \(\vec{r}_1\) is \(d \Omega_{21} = \frac{\hat{n}_2 \cdot \vec{r}_{12}}{|\vec{r}_{12}|^3} \, dS_2\). From the expression of the elementary power flux,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}
 d P_{1 \rightarrow 2} = I_{1}(\vec{r}_{12}, \hat{n}_1) \, \frac{\vec{r}_{12} }{|\vec{r}_{12}|^3} \cdot \hat{n}_2 \, dS_2 = \vec{s}_{12} \cdot \hat{n}_2 \, dS_2 \ ,
\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
it’s possible to find the expression of the power flux density vector
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\vec{s}_{12} = I_{12} \frac{\vec{r}_{12}}{|\vec{r}_{12}|^3} \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
For a Lambert medium then
\begin{equation}\label{equation:ch/heat-transfer-radiation:power-flux:radiant-intensity}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
 d P_{1 \rightarrow 2}
 & = I_{1}(\vec{r}_{12}, \hat{n}_1) \, \frac{\vec{r}_{12} }{|\vec{r}_{12}|^3} \cdot \hat{n}_2 \, dS_2 = \\
 & = I_{0,1} \frac{\vec{r}_{12}}{|\vec{r}_{12}|} \cdot \hat{n}_1 \, \frac{\vec{r}_{12} }{|\vec{r}_{12}|^3} \cdot \hat{n}_2 \, dS_2 \ .
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation}

\subsubsection{Stefan\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann law}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:stefan-boltzmann-law}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-radiation-stefan-boltzmann}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxstylestrong{radiant exitance}, \(M_e^\circ\), \sphinxhyphen{} i.e. the total power through an emisphere emitted by a source, per unit area of the source \sphinxhyphen{} of a black body at temperature \(T_1\) is
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}M_e^\circ = \sigma T_1^4 \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
with the \sphinxstylestrong{Stefan\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann constant}, \(\sigma = 5.67 \cdot 10^{-8} \frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^2 \text{K}^4}\).
\subsubsection*{Total radiation and Stefan\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann constant \protect\(\ \sigma\protect\)}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Integration over frequency \(f \in [0, +\infty]\), and over an emisphere receiver surface (or better, over the equivalent solid angle) for a Lambert medium provide the \sphinxstylestrong{radiant exitance}
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  M_{e}^\circ = M_{1,2^{emisphere}}
  & = \frac{d P_{1 \rightarrow 2}}{d S_1} = \\
  & = \int_{f=0}^{+\infty} \int_{\Omega_{emisphere}} I_{1,0}(f) \cos \theta_{12} \, df \, d\Omega_{2/1} = \\
  & = \int_{f=0}^{+\infty}I_{1,0}(f) \, df \, \int_{\phi = 0}^{2\pi} \int_{\theta=0}^{\pi_2} \cos \theta \sin \theta \, d \theta \, d\phi \ ,
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
with the solid angle with spherical coordinates, \(d\Omega_{2/1} = \sin \theta \, d \theta \, d\phi\). The geometric integral gives
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}
  \int_{\phi = 0}^{2\pi} \int_{\theta=0}^{\pi_2} \cos \theta_{} \, d \theta \, d\phi = \pi \ .
\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
For a black\sphinxhyphen{}body the integral over frequency gives
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  \int_{f = 0}^{+\infty} I_{1,0}(f) \, df
  & = \int_{f=0}^{+\infty} \frac{2 h f^3}{c^2} \frac{1}{e^{\frac{hf}{k_B T}}-1} \, df \\
  & = T^4 \frac{2 h}{c^2} \left(\frac{k_B}{h}\right)^4 \int_{u=0}^{+\infty} \frac{u^3}{e^u-1} \, du =: T^4 \frac{\sigma}{\pi} \ ,
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
i.e. it’s proportional to \(T^4\) with a constant of proportionality built on constants of nature, speed of light \(c\), Boltzmann constant \(k_B\), and Planck constant \(h\). The integral is non\sphinxhyphen{}dimensional and its value%
\begin{footnote}[2]\sphinxAtStartFootnote
\sphinxstylestrong{todo} Evaluate it
%
\end{footnote} is \(\frac{\pi^4}{15}\). \sphinxstylestrong{Stefan\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann constant} \(\sigma\) is then defined as
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\sigma := \pi \cdot \frac{2 h}{c^2} \left( \frac{k_B}{h} \right)^4 \int_{u=0}^{+\infty} \frac{u^3}{e^{u}-1} du = \frac{2 \pi^5}{15}\frac{k_B^4}{c^2 h^3} = 5.67 \cdot 10^{-8} \frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^2 \text{K}^4} \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Integration over a generic surface \(S_2\) of the power flux emitted by a source \(1\) per unit surface of the source gives
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  M_{12} = \frac{d P_{1 \rightarrow 2} }{d S_1} = T_1^4 \, \frac{\sigma}{\pi} \int_{S_2} \hat{n}_1 \cdot \frac{\vec{r}_{12}}{|\vec{r}_{12}|} \, \frac{\vec{r}_{12}}{|\vec{r}_{12}|^3} \cdot \hat{n}_2 \, dS_2
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Thus the elementary power per unit surface of the source \(1\) and the receiver \(2\) is
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}dP_{1 \rightarrow 2} = T_1^4 \, \frac{\sigma}{\pi} \, \hat{n}_1 \cdot \frac{\vec{r}_{12}}{|\vec{r}_{12}|} \, \frac{\vec{r}_{12}}{|\vec{r}_{12}|^3} \cdot \hat{n}_2 \, dS_1 \, dS_2 \end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Comparing this expression of the power flux from source \(1\) to the elementary surface \(d S_2\) with the expression \eqref{equation:ch/heat-transfer-radiation:power-flux:radiant-intensity} using the radiant intensity \(I_{0,1}\), it immediately follows that the elementary radiant intensity \(d I_{0,1}\) of the elementary surface \(d S_1\) of the source reads
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}d I_{0,1} = T_1^4 \, \frac{\sigma}{\pi} \, d S_1 \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}

\paragraph{Geometry effects on radiation}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:geometry-effects-on-radiation}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-radiation-stefan-boltzmann-geometry}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The elementary power is the product of \(\frac{\sigma T_1^4}{\pi}\) and a factor depending only on the geometry of the problem,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}d G_{12} := \hat{n}_1 \cdot \frac{\vec{r}_{12}}{|\vec{r}_{12}|} \, \frac{\vec{r}_{12}}{|\vec{r}_{12}|^3} \cdot \hat{n}_2 \, dS_1 \, dS_2 \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
This factor is \sphinxstylestrong{symmetric}, i.e. inverting the roles of the source and the receiver is irrelevant. Letting \(G_{12} = \int_{S_1} \int_{S_2} dG_{12}\), if the source surface \(1\) has uniform temperature \(T_1\) and the receiver surface has uniform temperature \(T_2\), (and assuming {\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-radiation-emittivity}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{emissvity}}}} \(\varepsilon = 1\), for black\sphinxhyphen{}bodies) thus the power fluxes from \(1\) to \(2\) and from \(2\) to \(1\) read
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  P_{1 \rightarrow 2} & = T_1^4 \frac{\sigma}{\pi} G_{12} \\
  P_{2 \rightarrow 1} & = T_2^4 \frac{\sigma}{\pi} G_{12} \ .
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Thus the net power into surfaces \(1\) and \(2\) are
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  P_{\rightarrow 1} & = P_{2 \rightarrow 1} - P_{1 \rightarrow 2} \\
  P_{\rightarrow 2} & = P_{1 \rightarrow 2} - P_{2 \rightarrow 1} \ ,
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
so that \(P_{\rightarrow 1} + P_{\rightarrow 2} = 0\).

\sphinxAtStartPar
In the limit of \sphinxstylestrong{small surfaces compared with their distance}, an approximate expression of the integral \(\int_{S_1} \int_{S_2} d G\) allows to write the power transmitted by radiation from surface \(1\) to surface \(2\) as
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  P_{1 \rightarrow 2} = \frac{\sigma T_1^4}{\pi} G_{12} 
    = \frac{\sigma T_1^4}{\pi} A_{1, \perp \vec{r}_{12}} \Omega_{2/1}
    = \frac{\sigma T_1^4}{\pi} A_{2, \perp \vec{r}_{12}} \Omega_{1/2}
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
i.e. as the product of the radiant … \sphinxstylestrong{todo} \sphinxstyleemphasis{find the right term} \(\frac{\sigma T_1^4}{\pi}\), the projection of the surface \(S_{1}\) in a plane orthogonal to the distance vector \(\vec{r}_{12}\) and the solid angle \(\Omega_{2/1}\) of the surface \(2\) as seen by surface \(1\), or viceversa.
\subsubsection*{Proof of the small surface limit}


\subsubsection{Emissivity and coefficient of absorption}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:emissivity-and-coefficient-of-absorption}}\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:classical-thermodynamics-heat-transmission-radiation-emittivity}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The radiation emission of a generic body differs from the radiation of a black\sphinxhyphen{}body. Its emittance can be written comparing its emission with that of of a black body. In general, the emission could differ as a function of the frequency of the radiation and the direction of the radiation.

\sphinxAtStartPar
…

\sphinxAtStartPar
If there’s no dependence on \(f\) and \(\hat{r}\) \sphinxhyphen{} or taking the average values, neglecting the dependence on \(f\) and \(\hat{r}\) if using the average coefficient \sphinxhyphen{} the radiant exitance is
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}M_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Reflection coefficient \(r\) and absorbtion (or transmission) coefficient \(a\) are then defined as the reflected and absorbed ratios of the incident radiation. Given incident radiation with power \(P_i\), power of reflected and absorbed radiation reads
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}P_r = r \, P_i \quad , \quad P_a = a \, P_i \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
and for “power balance”, the sum of absorbed and reflected power must be equal to the incident power
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}P_i = P_r + P_a \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
and thus the coefficients must satistfy the following relation \sphinxstylestrong{check} \sphinxstyleemphasis{check the definition of the coefficients, if they should take into account the emission…}
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}1 = r + a \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}\label{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:example-2}
\begin{sphinxadmonition}{note}{Example 9.3.1 (Energy exchange in isolated systems and relations between coefficients \sphinxhyphen{} \sphinxstylestrong{todo} \sphinxstylestrong{check})}



\sphinxAtStartPar
Radiation transmission is assumed to be instantaneous here: this assumption holds for bodies close enough for the time \(\Delta t = \frac{d_{12}}{c}\) to be small enough to be treated as zero.

\sphinxAtStartPar
A system composed of two bodies. Energy balance of a body immediately follows the first principle of thermodynamics: time derivative of energy of a system equals the net power done on the system. Here the net power equals the difference between the absorbed incoming power and the emitted power,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{cases} 
  \dot{E}_1 = P^a_{\rightarrow 1} - P_1^e \\
  \dot{E}_2 = P^a_{\rightarrow 2} - P_2^e
\end{cases}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Incoming power.} The incoming power is due to the power emitted and reflected by the other body,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{cases}
  P_{\rightarrow 1} = P_{2}^e + r_2 P_{\rightarrow 2} \\
  P_{\rightarrow 2} = P_{1}^e + r_1 P_{\rightarrow 1} 
\end{cases}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
From these two relations, the incoming powers can be explitly written as function of the emitted powers,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{cases}
  P_{\rightarrow 1} = \dfrac{P_2^e + r_2 P_1^e}{1 - r_1 r_2} \\
  P_{\rightarrow 2} = \dfrac{P_1^e + r_1 P_2^e}{1 - r_1 r_2}
\end{cases}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Energy balance of the system.} Energy of an isolated system is constant and thus the dime derivative of the energy is zero,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  0
  & = \dot{E} = \dot{E}_1 + \dot{E}_2 = \\
  & = a_1 \dfrac{P_2^e + r_2 P_1^e}{1 - r_1 r_2} - P_1^e + a_2 \dfrac{P_1^e + r_1 P_2^e}{1 - r_1 r_2} - P_2^e = \\ 
  & = P_1 \frac{a_2 + a_1 r_2 - 1 + r_1 r_2}{1 - r_1 r_2} + P_2 \frac{a_1 + a_2 r_1 - 1 + r_1 r_2}{1 - r_1 r_2} \\ 
  & = T_1^4 \sigma \varepsilon_1 \frac{a_2 + a_1 r_2 - 1 + r_1 r_2}{1 - r_1 r_2} + T_2^4 \sigma \varepsilon_2 \frac{a_1 + a_2 r_1 - 1 + r_1 r_2}{1 - r_1 r_2} \ . 
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
If this relation must hold in any condition, for any value of the emitted powers and for any independent choice of the materials of the bodies, the coefficients must be zero
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{cases}
  a_2 + a_1 r_2 - 1 + r_1 r_2 = 0 \\
  a_1 + a_2 r_1 - 1 + r_1 r_2 = 0 \\
\end{cases}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Subtracting,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}a_2 (1 - r_1) = a_1 (1 - r_2) \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
and, since the relation must hold for any choice of pair of materials, the relation \(a_1 = 1 - r_1\) is found again.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Bodies in thermal equlibrium.} If each body is in thermal equilibrium,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{cases}
  0 = \dfrac{P_2^e + r_2 P^{e}_1}{ 1 - r_1 r_2} - P_1^e = \sigma G_{12} \dfrac{ \varepsilon_2 T_2^4 + ( r_2 - 1 + r_1 r_2 ) \varepsilon_1 T_1^4 }{ 1 - r_1 r_2 } \\
  0 = \dfrac{P_1^e + r_1 P^{e}_2}{ 1 - r_1 r_2} - P_2^e = \sigma G_{12} \dfrac{ \varepsilon_1 T_1^4 + ( r_1 - 1 + r_1 r_2 ) \varepsilon_2 T_2^4 }{ 1 - r_1 r_2 } \\
\end{cases}\end{split}
\end{equation*}\end{sphinxadmonition}


\paragraph{Kirchhoff’s law}
\label{\detokenize{ch/heat-transfer-radiation:kirchhoff-s-law}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
For systems in thermodynamic equilibrium,…
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
constant ratio of…

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\(\alpha = \varepsilon\)? for a system in thermal equilibrium \sphinxstylestrong{only}

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope

\chapter{Principles of Thermodynamics \sphinxhyphen{} Electro\sphinxhyphen{}chemical systems}
\label{\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical:principles-of-thermodynamics-electro-chemical-systems}}\label{\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical:physics-thermodynamics-foundation-principles-electro-chemical}}\label{\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In this section:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Introduction to {\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical:physics-thermodynamics-foundation-principles-electro-chemical-reactions}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{chemical reactions}}}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical:physics-thermodynamics-foundation-principles-electro-chemical-first-principle}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{Energy balance}}}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar

\section{Chemical reactions}
\label{\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical:chemical-reactions}}\label{\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical:physics-thermodynamics-foundation-principles-electro-chemical-reactions}}\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}a A + b B \rightleftharpoons c C + d D \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
or
\begin{equation}\label{equation:ch/principles-electrochemical:eq:reaction:stoichiometry}
\begin{split}\nu_{R_i}  R_i \rightleftharpoons \nu_{P_j} P_j\end{split}
\end{equation}

\section{First principle \sphinxhyphen{} energy balance}
\label{\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical:first-principle-energy-balance}}\label{\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical:physics-thermodynamics-foundation-principles-electro-chemical-first-principle}}\subsubsection*{Energy balance}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Total energy balance
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}d E^{tot} = \delta L^{ext} + \delta Q^{ext} \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Macroscopic kinetic energy balance (theorem, from mechanics)
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}d K = \delta L^{ext} + \delta L^{int} \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Internal energy \(E := E^{tot} - K\)
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}d E = - \delta L^{int} + \delta Q^{ext} \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}\subsubsection*{Internal energy, intensive and extensive variables}
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}d E = - \delta L^{int,rev} + \underbrace{\delta^+ D + \delta Q^{ext}}_{=:T dS}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
As \(dE\) is a state function, and \(\delta L^{int,rev}\) must be reversible by definition, the remaining terms must be reversible as well and completing the exact differential of the energy as a state function. The internal nature may have different natures, e.g. mechanical, elelctrical, magnetic, chemical
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\delta L^{int,rev} = \delta L^{int,mech} + \delta L^{int,el} + \delta L^{int,magn} + \delta L^{int,el} + \dots\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
but all of these works can be written as the product of a intensive variable \(\mathbf{F}\) of the system and the variation of a extensive variable \(\mathbf{X}\), formally
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\delta L^{int,rev} = \mathbf{F} \cdot d \mathbf{X} \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
mechanical work, depends on the nature of the system. As an example, for a gas \(\delta L^{int,rev} = - P d V\), or for an elastic solid \(\delta L^{int,rev} = - V \sigma : d \varepsilon\)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
electrical work, being \(V\) the (uniform) electrical potential of the system, and \(Q\) its electrical charge (see \sphinxhref{https://basics2022.github.io/bbooks-physics-electromagnetism/ch/regimes-electrostatics.html\#systems-with-uniform-potential}{Elecetrostatic energy: systems with uniform potential})
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}- V d Q\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
chemical work, with \(\mu_i\) the molar chemical potential \(i\) of the substance (the amount of internal energy added to the system by a change in the number of moles of the \(i\) substance in the system)
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\mu_i d N_i\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{todo} See and uniform the treatment in \DUrole{xref,myst}{First principle}, \DUrole{xref,myst}{Second principle}, \DUrole{xref,myst}{Gibbs formalization}, {\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/potentials:classical-thermodynamics-potentials}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{Thermodynamic potentials}}}}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Using \(\mathbf{X}, S\) as independent variables,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}d E = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{X}, S) \cdot d \mathbf{X} + T(\mathbf{X}, S) d S \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
It the energy of the system is a order\sphinxhyphen{}1 homogeneous function of the extensive variables of the system, \(E(\lambda \mathbf{Y}) = \lambda E(\mathbf{Y})\)%
\begin{footnote}[1]\sphinxAtStartFootnote
Not all the systems are made by sub\sphinxhyphen{}systems that combine “extensively”: it’s likely to be impossible to write the energy of these systems as a homogeneous function of the extensive variables of the system.
%
\end{footnote}, Euler theorem ({\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/potentials:thm-euler}]{\sphinxcrossref{Theorem 3.1.1}}}) gives
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}E = T S + \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{X} \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Explicitly writing the number of moles of the substances of the system out fror the extensive variables \(\mathbf{X}\), \(\mathbf{X} \rightarrow \left( \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{N} \right)\), and the chemical potentials out from the generalized intensive variables \(\mathbf{F} \rightarrow \left( \mathbf{F}, \boldsymbol\mu \right)\), the expression of the internal energy and its differential reads
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
   E & = T S + \boldsymbol\mu \cdot \mathbf{N} + \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{X} \\
  dE & = T dS + \boldsymbol\mu \cdot d\mathbf{N} + \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{X} \ .
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}

\section{Law of Mass Action}
\label{\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical:law-of-mass-action}}\label{\detokenize{ch/principles-electrochemical:physics-thermodynamics-foundation-principles-electro-chemical-law-of-mass-action}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The law of mass action states that the forward velocity \(v_f\) (\(R \rightarrow P\)) and backward velocity \(v_b\) (\(R \leftarrow P\)) of reaction \eqref{equation:ch/principles-electrochemical:eq:reaction:stoichiometry} reads
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  v_f & := -\frac{1}{\nu_{R_k}}\frac{d [ R_k ]_f}{dt} :=  \frac{1}{\nu_{P_k}}\frac{d [ P_k ]_f}{dt} = k_f \prod_i [ R_i ]^{\alpha_{R_i}} \\ 
  v_r & :=  \frac{1}{\nu_{R_k}}\frac{d [ R_k ]_r}{dt} := -\frac{1}{\nu_{P_k}}\frac{d [ P_k ]_r}{dt} = k_r \prod_i [ P_i ]^{\alpha_{P_i}} \\ 
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Taking \(R \rightarrow P\) as the positive direction of the reaction, the velocity of the reaction is defined as \(v = v_r - v_f\), and the rate of change of the concentrations thus reads
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  \frac{d [ R_k ]}{dt} = \frac{d [ R_k ]_f}{dt} + \frac{d [ R_k ]_r}{dt} = \nu_{R_k} \left( v_r - v_f \right) = \nu_{R_k} \, v \\ 
  \frac{d [ P_k ]}{dt} = \frac{d [ P_k ]_f}{dt} + \frac{d [ P_k ]_r}{dt} = \nu_{P_k} \left( v_f - v_r \right) = \nu_{P_k} \, v \ .
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Equilibrium} is reached when \(v_r = v_f\), and thus the rate of change of concentration of the substances is zero.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Extent of reaction} \(\xi\), so that \(d \xi = v dt\).

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Constant \(k_r\) and \(k_f\) usually can be written as
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}k = A \exp \left( - \frac{E}{kT} \right) \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
being \(E\) the \sphinxstylestrong{activation energy}, \(k\) Boltzmann constant, \(T\) the temperature, and \(A\) a constant summarizing all the other conditions for the reaction to occur.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{References}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxurl{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical\_equilibrium}

\end{itemize}


\bigskip\hrule\bigskip


\sphinxstepscope

\sphinxAtStartPar
…
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ - RT \ln a_i \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}

\sphinxAtStartPar
derivation:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
with classical thermodynamics (for mixture of ideal gas, and then correct with the activity for the deviation from this behavior)

with statistical mechanics

\chapter{Batteries: thermo\sphinxhyphen{}electro\sphinxhyphen{}chemical modeling}
\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries:batteries-thermo-electro-chemical-modeling}}\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries:electro-chemical-batteries}}\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries::doc}}

\section{Introduction and working principles}
\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries:introduction-and-working-principles}}\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries:electro-chemical-batteries-intro}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A battery is an electrochemical system that converts chemical energy directly into electrical energy via spontaneous reduction\sphinxhyphen{}oxidation (redox) reactions. It acts as an electrochemical reactor where the spatial separation of the oxidation and reduction steps forces electrons to flow through an external load to perform work.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Core components.}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Anode (negative electrode): the electrode where oxidation (loss of electrons) occurs during discharge.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Cathode (positive electrode): the electrode where reduction (gain of electrons) occurs during discharge.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Electrolyte: a medium containing mobile ions that carries ionic current inside the cell while remaining electronically insulating.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Separator: a permeable membrane that prevents physical contact between the anode and cathode to avoid internal short circuits.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Classification.}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Primary (non\sphinxhyphen{}rechargeable): the underlying chemical reactions are thermodynamically irreversible or physically difficult to reverse. Once the reactants are depleted, the battery cannot be recharged (e.g., Alkaline, Zinc\sphinxhyphen{}carbon).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Secondary (rechargeable): the redox reactions are highly reversible. Applying an external electrical potential drives the current in reverse, regenerating the initial chemical reactants (e.g., Lithium\sphinxhyphen{}ion, Lead\sphinxhyphen{}acid, NiMH).

\end{itemize}


\section{Examples}
\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries:examples}}\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries:electro-chemical-batteries-examples}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:electro-chemical-batteries-daniell}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{Daniell cell}}}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/batteries-li:electro-chemical-batteries-li}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{Li\sphinxhyphen{}ion bettery}}}}

\end{itemize}



\sphinxstepscope


\section{Daniell cell}
\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:daniell-cell}}\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:electro-chemical-batteries-daniell}}\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell::doc}}

\subsection{Description}
\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:description}}\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:electro-chemical-batteries-daniell-description}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The Daniell cell consists of two separate half\sphinxhyphen{}cells:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
A zinc (\(\text{Zn}\)) electrode immersed in a zinc sulfate (\(\text{ZnSO}_4\)) aqueous solution, with ions \(Zn^{2+}\), \(SO_4^{2-}\) sully dissolved in that

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
A copper (\(\text{Cu}\)) electrode immersed in a copper(II) sulfate (\(\text{CuSO}_4\)) aqueous solution, ions \(Cu^{2+}\), \(SO_4^{2-}\) fully dissolved in that

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
A porous barrier (or salt bridge) separating the solutions to prevent rapid mixing while allowing sulfate ions (\(\text{SO}_4^{2-}\)) to migrate, between the 2 half\sphinxhyphen{}cells.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The spontanoues half\sphinxhyphen{}cell reactions during disharge are:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
at the anode:
\begin{equation}\label{equation:ch/batteries-daniell:eq:daniell:reaction:anode}
\begin{split}\text{Zn}(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + 2 e^-(s)\end{split}
\end{equation}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
at the cathode:
\begin{equation}\label{equation:ch/batteries-daniell:eq:daniell:reaction:cathode}
\begin{split}\text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) + 2 e^-(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Cu}(s)\end{split}
\end{equation}
\end{itemize}


\subsection{Regimes}
\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:regimes}}\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:electro-chemical-batteries-daniell-regimes}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:electro-chemical-batteries-daniell-regimes-open}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{Open circuit equilibrium}}}}: chemical reactions at equilibrium, no net motion of charges through the electrolytes and the salt bridge

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:electro-chemical-batteries-daniell-regimes-load}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{Operation under load}}}}. Chemical kinetics reactions and diffusion process of ions may produce bottlenecks in the process, and thus overpotentials and/or losses

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{Open\sphinxhyphen{}circuit equilibrium}
\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:open-circuit-equilibrium}}\label{\detokenize{ch/batteries-daniell:electro-chemical-batteries-daniell-regimes-open}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Internal energy}
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}dE = - P dV + T dS + \sum_i \mu_i d N_i + \sum_k \phi_k d Q_k\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
with \(i\) spanning all the substances involved in a reaction, and \(k\) all the regions with uniform electric potential,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  i & \in \{ \text{Zn}, \text{Zn}^{2+}, \text{Cu}^{2+}, \text{Cu} \} \\
  k & \in \{ \text{anode}, \text{cathode}, \text{electrolytes+bridge} \}
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Here the chemical potentials include both the energy changes associated with reaction and phase transformation.
Using the reactions \eqref{equation:ch/batteries-daniell:eq:daniell:reaction:anode}, \eqref{equation:ch/batteries-daniell:eq:daniell:reaction:cathode}, it follows (with \(z = 2\), number of electrons per reacting ion)
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  d Q_{\text{anode}}   & = - z q d n_{\text{Zn}^{2+}} =  - z q N_{A} d N_{\text{Zn}^{2+}} = - z F d N_{\text{Zn}^{2+}} \\ 
  d Q_{\text{cathode}} & = - z q d n_{\text{Cu}^{2+}} =  - z q N_{A} d N_{\text{Cu}^{2+}} = - z F d N_{\text{Cu}^{2+}} \ , 
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
having introduced the \sphinxstylestrong{Faraday’s constant}
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}F = q N_A = 96 485.33 \frac{\text{s} \text{A}}{\text{mol}} \ ,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
defined as the product of the elementary charge (electron charge, but positive), and the number of Avogadro \(N_A\).

\sphinxAtStartPar
If the motion of \(\text{SO}_4^{2-}\) ions through the salt bridge keeps the electrolytes electrically neutral, it’s possible to write the variation of the moles of substances in the system as a function of an \sphinxstylestrong{extent of reaction variable}, \(\xi\)
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}d N_{\text{Zn}^{2+}} = d N_{\text{SO}_4^{2-}, a} = - d N_{\text{SO}_4^{2-}, c} = - dN_{\text{Cu}^{2+}} = d \xi \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
If the electrolytes and the salt bridge are in an equi\sphinxhyphen{}potential region, then the motion of the \(\text{SO}_2^{2-}\) ions doesn’t influence the variation of the internal energy, that can be written as
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  d E
  & = - P dV + T dS + \left( \mu_{\text{Zn}^{2+}} - \mu_{\text{Zn}} - \mu_{\text{Cu}^{2+}} + \mu_{\text{Cu}} \right) d \xi - z F \phi_{a} d \xi + z F \phi_c d \xi = \\
  & = - P dV + T dS + \left[ \left( \mu_{\text{Zn}^{2+}} - \mu_{\text{Zn}} - \mu_{\text{Cu}^{2+}} + \mu_{\text{Cu}} \right) - z F \left( \phi_{a} - \phi_c \right) \right] d \xi \ .
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Gibbs’ free energy}, \(G = E + PV - TS\).
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}d G = V dP + S dT + \sum_i \mu_i dN_i - \sum_k \phi_k d Q_k\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Equilibrium condition \(d G = 0\) (see \DUrole{xref,myst}{Thermodynamic equilibrium: Gibbs’s free energy} \sphinxstylestrong{todo}) for constant \(P\) and \(T\) (and thus \(d P = 0\), \(d T = 0\)), requires that the electro\sphinxhyphen{}chemical force must be zero, i.e.
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}0 = \mu_{\text{Zn}^{2+}} - \mu_{\text{Zn}} - \mu_{\text{Cu}^{2+}} + \mu_{\text{Cu}} - z F \left( \phi_{a} - \phi_c \right) \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Thus, the electrostatic potential at open\sphinxhyphen{}circuit equilibrium between the cathode (positive electrode) and the anode (negative electrode) reads
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}V_{oc} = \phi_c - \phi_a = \frac{1}{z F} \left( - \mu_{\text{Zn}^{2+}} + \mu_{\text{Zn}} + \mu_{\text{Cu}^{2+}} - \mu_{\text{Cu}} \right)\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Introducing \DUrole{xref,myst}{Nernst equation} for the chemical potentials, the open\sphinxhyphen{}circuit potential can be written as
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\begin{aligned}
  V_{oc} 
  & = \frac{1}{zF} \sum_i \left\{ \text{sign}_i \mu_i + R T \ln a_i \right\} = \\
  & = \underbrace{ \frac{1}{zF} \left\{ - \mu_{\text{Zn}^{2+}}^\circ + \mu_{\text{Zn}}^\circ + \mu_{\text{Cu}^{2+}}^\circ - \mu_{\text{Cu}}^\circ \right\} }_{ = V_{oc}^\circ} + R T \ln \left( \frac{a_{\text{Cu}^{2+}} a_{\text{Zn}}}{a_{\text{Zn}^{2+}} a_{\text{Cu}}} \right) \\
  V_{oc} & = V_{oc}^\circ + R T \ln \left( \frac{a_{\text{Cu}^{2+}}}{a_{\text{Zn}^{2+}}} \right) \ ,
\end{aligned}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
as the activity of solid phases are equal to \(1\) by definition.%
\begin{footnote}[1]\sphinxAtStartFootnote
By definition the standard state of a solid is chosen to be the pure solid at the system temperature and a standard pressure. From this and Nernst equation, it immediately follows that \(a = 1\) for the solid in the reference condition. From a more intuitive point of view, concentration of a solid doesn’t change: adding a piece of a solid medium doesn’t make the atoms in the crystal more packed, and thus the chemical environment around an atom of the solid lattice doesn’t change, and thus its chemical potential neither.
%
\end{footnote} The activity of the dissolved ions are related (but not equal or proportional, as the behavior of a highly concentrated solution with charged ions deviates significantly from the ideal behavior) to the concentration of the ions at the interface close to the electrode, the regions where the reactions occur,
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}a_i = \gamma_i \frac{C_i}{C_i^\circ} \ .\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxhref{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Debye\%E2\%80\%93H\%C3\%BCckel\_theory}{Debye\sphinxhyphen{}Huckel theory} provides a model for the activity coefficient \(\gamma_i\), as a function of the ionic strength of the solution.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The standard open\sphinxhyphen{}circuit cell potential of a Daniell cell is \(V_{oc}^\circ = \phi_c^\circ - \phi_a^\circ = 1.102 \text{V}\)
